sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The grid has 3 panels - left (7×4), right-top (3×4), right-bottom (3×4). There are 5 green cells total. I've only clicked green cells in the left panel so far. Let me try clicking a green cell in the right panel.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 7 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: I clicked on the green cell at (19, 36) which is the third green cell in the left panel (at column 35-38). This is the same cell I clicked before that reset everything! Let me check what happened this time.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking the green cell at (19, 36)
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation

print("Before - G/g objects:")
for n in before_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('G', 'g'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, top-left=({min_r},{min_c})")

print("\nAfter - G/g objects:")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('G', 'g'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, top-left=({min_r},{min_c})")

# Check changed cells
before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nChanged cells:")
changes = []
for i in range(len(before_ascii)):
    if i < len(after_ascii):
        for j in range(len(before_ascii[i])):
            if j < len(after_ascii[i]):
                if before_ascii[i][j] != after_ascii[i][j]:
                    changes.append((i, j, before_ascii[i][j], after_ascii[i][j]))
for c in changes:
    print(f"  ({c[0]},{c[1]}): '{c[2]}' -> '{c[3]}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so clicking on the green cell at (19, 36) only changed the HUD timer (cell (0,6) from 'W' to 'w'). No G marker appeared, no gray cells were transformed. This is different from when I clicked the green cells at (1, 1) and (1, 2) earlier.
Wait, but I previously clicked (19, 36) and it reset everything. Let me check the history to see what happened before.
Actually, looking back at the history:
1. First click was on (1, 1) - but that was a grid coordinate, not physical. Let me check what physical coordinates I actually clicked.
2. The first executed action was MOUSE(row=19, col=18) which clicked the first green cell in the left panel.
3. The second was MOUSE(row=19, col=24) which clicked the second green cell.
4. The third was MOUSE(row=19, col=36) which clicked the third green cell and reset everything.
5. The fourth was MOUSE(row=19, col=30) which clicked a gray cell and reset with animation.
6. The fifth was ACTION7 which advanced the timer.
7. The sixth was MOUSE(row=19, col=36) which... only advanced the timer?
Wait, this is strange. The third click on (19, 36) reset everything, but the sixth click on the same coordinates only advanced the timer. Let me check if the board state was different before these clicks.
Before the third click, there was a G marker and g diamonds from the previous clicks. Before the sixth click, the board was in the original state (no G marker, no g diamonds).
So maybe:
- Clicking a green cell when there's no G marker places a G marker and transforms cells
- Clicking a green cell when there's already a G marker moves the G marker and transforms different cells
- Clicking a green cell when there are 2+ G markers resets everything
- Clicking a green cell when the board is already reset just advances the timer (because it's the 3rd green cell in the row, and clicking it as the 3rd selection resets)
Wait, but the sixth click only advanced the timer. Let me reconsider.
Actually, I think the issue is that I clicked (19, 36) which is the center of the third green cell in the left panel. But maybe the click didn't register as a "game" click because the board was already in the reset state. Or maybe clicking the same cell twice in a row does something different.
Let me try clicking on a green cell in the right panel (at (25, 42) or (37, 42)) to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Clicking (19, 36) again only advanced the timer, not the game state. The green cell at (19, 36) previously reset the board when there was an active G marker, but now does nothing special. Let me try clicking a green cell in the actual right panel.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the green cell in the top-right panel at (25, 42)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 42}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
